TextENGNew: There was no wake-up chime.
changeReason: OLD adds subjective 'I didn't hear' not in JPN; NEW correctly states the factual absence of the chime (起床のチャイムはない).

TextENGNew: I'm not at the academy anymore.
changeReason: JPN 学園 specifically means 'academy' (referring to St. Lucia's boarding school); 'school' is less precise and loses the institutional connotation.

TextENGNew: My sleep-addled brain understood that much.
And yet, waking up at exactly the time the academy's prescribed rising hour dictated was deeply irritating.
changeReason: OLD paraphrases loosely ('doesn't feel good'); NEW captures the JPN nuance of シャクだった (deeply irritating) and the specific institutional schedule.

TextENGNew: I considered going back to sleep, but once I was awake, there was no undoing it.
changeReason: OLD omits Shion's deliberation about going back to sleep (寝直そうかとも思ったが) present in JPN; NEW includes it.

TextENGNew: Besides, from now on I wouldn't be trudging through a life dictated by others, grumbling all the while.
changeReason: OLD paraphrases as 'torturous school life'; NEW more accurately captures JPN meaning of no longer passively following a life dictated by others while complaining.

TextENGNew: Whether my days were joyful or dull -- that was entirely up to me now.
Ahh! That's actually pretty uplifting.@k This is what it feels like to be alive.
changeReason: OLD adds 'for the first time in a while' not present in JPN; also あっは☆ is more like 'Ahh!' than 'Yay!'.

TextENGNew: With no change of clothes to speak of, I was still wearing the academy track suit.
...Supposedly designed by some Italian designer or another -- but I had serious doubts about where exactly the 'design' was in something this bland and flavorless.
changeReason: OLD heavily truncates the JPN which discusses the supposed Italian designer and questions where any 'design' exists in something so bland; NEW captures this skepticism fully.

TextENGNew: Here I was, savoring my freedom, and yet being stuck in school-issued clothes left a distinctly unpleasant taste.
changeReason: OLD adds 'I want to enjoy my freedom as soon as possible' not in JPN; NEW more accurately captures the contrast between savoring freedom and being stuck in school clothes.

TextENGNew: I certainly couldn't walk around outside dressed like this.
I'd have Kasai bring me something proper to wear later.
After that, maybe I'd think about how to arrange the room.
changeReason: JPN 部屋のレイアウト means room layout/arrangement; OLD's 'redecorating' implies changing decor rather than planning the arrangement of an empty room.

TextENGNew: This was going to be my castle from now on, after all.
The apartment was in a fairly impressive high-rise on the outskirts of Okinomiya --@k a property that a real estate company in my father's network had 'seized' through some murky dispute or another.
changeReason: NEW adds details present in JPN: 高層マンション (high-rise), the property was 'seized' (差し押さえた) through murky circumstances; OLD simplifies to 'obtained after foreclosure.'

TextENGNew: ...Most of the original residents had cleared out because of all that murky business.
So despite the abundance of units, it was a lonely building with scarcely any sign of life.
Apart from the manager couple and maybe one or two other households, everyone living there was connected to my father -- and none of them looked particularly respectable.
changeReason: JPN 管理人夫婦 specifies 'manager couple' (married pair); OLD renders as just 'the manager,' omitting the spouse.

TextENGNew: It probably wouldn't be long before the last decent residents were driven out...
The unit I'd be living in had apparently served as a model room, so it had the look and feel of a business hotel suite.
The furniture was halfway there, but nothing actually needed for daily life was present.
changeReason: JPN 中途半端に家具は揃っている means furniture was incompletely/half-heartedly furnished; OLD's 'fully furnished' is the opposite meaning.

TextENGNew: I'd pick those up little by little as I went.
``...That's actually better, isn't it?@k@vS06/06/150600072. It'll make the place feel like it's really mine.``
I nodded along to my own words.
It probably looked silly, but morale boosts matter.
At the academy, the only television had been in the common area, and commercial channels were forbidden.
changeReason: JPN 民放 means commercial/private broadcast channels, not 'cable channels' as in OLD; NEW correctly uses 'commercial channels.'

TextENGNew: As an act of pure emancipation, I flipped through every commercial channel I could find.
Of course, at this ungodly hour, none of them were airing anything worth watching.
Still, I managed to enjoy it well enough, and even the commercials -- which I hadn't seen in ages -- had a certain charm.
changeReason: OLD omits that Shion hadn't seen commercials in a long time (久しぶりに見る), which emphasizes her isolation at the academy.

TextENGNew: Once I'd tired of that and my stomach started growling, Kasai arrived with a boxed meal.
changeReason: OLD omits that Shion first got bored of TV then got hungry (JPN sequence); also JPN 弁当 = boxed meal, not specifically 'lunch.'

TextENGNew: ``Good morning, Shion-san.@k@vS06/15/151500069. Did you sleep well last night?``

``Sleeping on the couch wrapped in a blanket was pretty rough.@k@vS06/06/150600074. A proper Japanese futon on the floor -- that's what I need.@k@vS06/06/150600075. Mmm, this pickled radish is delicious~!``
changeReason: OLD omits 毛布 (blanket) from JPN; also NEW better captures the casual conversational tone of the original.

TextENGNew: ``Glad to hear it.@k@vS06/15/151500071. Let me know what you need later.@k@vS06/15/151500072. I'll do my best to get everything arranged.``

``Thanks.@k@vS06/06/150600077. Oh, but don't go overboard worrying about me.@k@vS06/06/150600078. This is the life I chose, after all.@k@vS06/06/150600079. Besides, your wallet would give out if I started bleeding you dry! Ahahahahaha!``
Money, and the reality of my new life going forward -- these were things I needed to address seriously.
changeReason: OLD vaguely says 'my plan'; NEW specifies money and future life (お金とこれからの生活) as in JPN.

TextENGNew: I couldn't keep leaning on Kasai's wallet forever.

``I'm thinking of getting a part-time job.@k@vS06/06/150600081. I should at least earn my own living expenses.@k@vS06/06/150600082. This is the life I chose -- taking responsibility for it is only natural!``
``That's an admirable attitude.@k@vS06/15/151500074. But are you sure about that?@k@vS06/15/151500075. If you go out in public, it's only a matter of time before a family member spots you.``

``I've already thought about that.@k@vS06/06/150600084. That's where Mion comes in.@k@vS06/06/150600085. Kasai, would you call the main house for me?@k@vS06/06/150600086. When Mion picks up, hand me the receiver.``
``Good grief, what are you scheming now?@k@vS06/15/151500077. What's the plan?``
changeReason: OLD loses JPN 企んでる (scheming); 'whatever you say' doesn't convey Kasai's wry suspicion about Shion's plan.

TextENGNew: Kasai asked as he dialed the main house.
changeReason: OLD omits that Kasai was asking a question while dialing (JPN 聞いてくる); NEW includes this detail.

TextENGNew: It was rather ironic -- I needed to avoid the main house's notice at all costs, yet the very first call I was making was to the main house itself.

``Good morning.@k@vS06/15/151500079. ......Is this Mion-san?@k@vS06/15/151500080. ......It's been a while. This is Kasai.@k@vS06/15/151500081. ...Yes.@k@vS06/15/151500082. Good morning.@k@vS06/15/151500083. ...Could you hold for just a moment?@k@vS06/15/151500084. I'm passing the phone to someone.``
I took the receiver from Kasai.
``Can you guess who this is?``
``Th-... That voice!!@k@vS06/03/150300003. But that's impossible...!@k@vS06/03/150300004. Private calls are forbidden at Shion's dorm...!@k@vS06/03/150300005. It can't be...!``

``Outside calls are strictly forbidden at the academy, you know.@k@vS06/06/150600091. ...And yet here I am, calling you. Heh heh heh!@k@vS06/06/150600092. Even my dim-witted big sister can figure out what that means.@k@vS06/06/150600093. Heh heh heh!``
``...You... you broke out of the school?!@k@vS06/03/150300007. ...H-... How did you...?!``
``Never mind that.@k@vS06/06/150600095. ...I missed you so much, Sis...``
If that was my limit, it was Mion's limit too.
````Pfft. ......Heh heh heh... ...BWAHAHAHAHAHAHAHA!!!````
Neither of us could keep up the melodrama any longer, and we both dissolved into laughter.
...Through the receiver, the two sisters shared their first real, belly-deep laugh together in a very long time.
``It's been forever, Shion!!@k@vS06/03/150300011. What on earth happened?!``
``What do you think happened?@k@vS06/06/150600099. I wasn't lying about any of it.``
changeReason: JPN どうしたもこうしたも is an idiomatic dismissive expression; OLD's 'There's not much else to it' misses the tone; NEW captures the dismissiveness better.

TextENGNew: ``Wait... so you actually escaped?!@k@vS06/03/150300013. ...Wow... you've got guts...``
``Their security was a little too lax to keep me locked up!@k@vS06/06/150600101. Ahaha!``
``...Maybe the school should be grateful you went quietly...@k@vS06/03/150300015. If you'd been serious, you could've blown the whole place sky-high on your way out.``
``Bombing might be a stretch, but I could've managed a nice little arson.@k@vS06/06/150600103. Ahahahahaha!``
Kasai watched the sisters enthusiastically bonding over violent scenarios, wearing an expression that was equal parts exasperation and amusement.
changeReason: NEW captures the JPN nuance of 物騒な話題で盛り上がる (bonding over dangerous topics) that OLD simplifies; also OLD uses 'us' when JPN uses third-person 姉妹.

TextENGNew: ``Sis, is it okay to talk right now?``
``It's fine.@k@vS06/03/150300017. Granny's out at a lesson.@k@vS06/03/150300018. She said she won't be back until evening.``
``Perfect.@k@vS06/06/150600106. Let's meet up and talk properly soon.@k@vS06/06/150600107. For today, the phone will have to do.``
``Okay.``
``Has the main house gotten any word from the academy about my escape?``
changeReason: JPN asks about 本家 (main house) receiving word from the academy; OLD changes this to 'grandmother' specifically, losing the institutional question.

TextENGNew: ``Nope.@k@vS06/03/150300021. Nothing.@k@vS06/03/150300022. All calls and visitors to the main house go through me now, so nothing reaches Granny without my knowing.@k@vS06/03/150300023. I can say with confidence that she hasn't heard a thing.``
``...Wow.@k@vS06/06/150600110. That's impressive.@k@vS06/06/150600111. Spoken like the true next head of the Sonozaki family.``
``I hate all that, though. You should take over for me.``

``You're the one possessed by the demon, aren't you?@k@vS06/06/150600113. Well, do your best.@k@vS06/06/150600114. I'll be cheering you on from the shadows.@k@vS06/06/150600115. Anyway, that doesn't matter right now. Ahaha!``
``That's such a detached way to put it...@k@vS06/03/150300026. Well, whatever...``
``If anything comes up about me, contact Kasai, okay?@k@vS06/06/150600117. I'd like to give you my number here, but... I'll keep it secret for now.@k@vS06/06/150600118. Sorry.``

``Yeah...@k@vS06/03/150300028. If Granny finds out you ran away from the academy... things are going to get messy.@k@vS06/03/150300029. Got it.@k@vS06/03/150300030. If anything happens, I'll pass it along to Kasai-san.``
``Thanks.@k@vS06/06/150600120. Also, I have a favor to ask, Sis.``
``I figured that was coming.@k@vS06/03/150300032. Just so we're clear -- money's off the table!``
``I wasn't delusional enough to think I could mooch money off you.@k@vS06/06/150600122. What I need is a little help with my part-time job.``
``Ahh, I see.@k@vS06/03/150300034. ...So that's the angle.@k@vS06/03/150300035. Specifically, what do you need me to do?``
In situations like this, communication between us sisters is lightning fast.
We know what the other is thinking instantly, so we can skip the explanations and cut straight to the point.
``Uncle Yoshiroh was always on my side, right?@k@vS06/06/150600124. I'm going to ask him if I can work at one of his shops.@k@vS06/06/150600125. Once my shifts are set, I'll let you know.``
``So I just need to make myself scarce during your work hours. Got it.@k@vS06/03/150300037. ...What a hassle, though.``
``Oh, come on, Sis.@k@|@y@vS06/06/150600127. You'd really turn down a request from your own twin sister?@k@vS06/06/150600128. Seriously?``
changeReason: JPN 双子の妹 specifically means 'twin younger sister'; OLD renders as 'only sister,' losing the twin reference important to the Sonozaki twins' identity.

TextENGNew: ``Fine, fine -- I'll do it!@k@vS06/03/150300039. I know better than to deal with the fallout if I turned you down.``
``Ahaha!@k@vS06/06/150600130. Thank you!@k@vS06/06/150600131. Next time, I'll treat you to cake from Schlanke Bertha's!@k@vS06/06/150600132. I'll stuff you full of adorable little tarts~!``
``...Oh, Shion.@k@vS06/03/150300041. Schlanke Bertha's... it closed down last fall.``

``Yeah.@k@vS06/03/150300043. The owner had a stroke.@k@vS06/03/150300044. He reopened once after getting out of the hospital,@k@vS06/03/150300045. but his health just couldn't hold up.@k@vS06/03/150300046. ...Everyone was sad to see it go, but the shop had to close.``
changeReason: OLD omits 惜しまれたけど (everyone mourned/regretted it) present in JPN; NEW includes this community sentiment about the shop closing.

TextENGNew: ``...I see.@k@vS06/06/150600136. ...That's a shame.@k@vS06/06/150600137. ...I guess my dream of eating their Mont Blanc until I burst will stay just that -- a dream forever.``
What stung wasn't simply that my favorite cake shop had closed.
It was the loneliness of realizing that the town had gone on changing without me while I was away.
changeReason: JPN 寂しさ means 'loneliness/sadness'; OLD renders as 'regrettable,' a different emotion; NEW correctly identifies the feeling as loneliness.

TextENGNew: ``...Are there other things that have changed? Things I don't know about?``
``Yeah.@k@vS06/03/150300048. A few things.@k@vS06/03/150300049. I'll tell you everything when we meet up.``
``Thanks.@k@vS06/06/150600140. I'll share everything about life at the academy too.@k@vS06/06/150600141. Let's find a time soon.@k@vS06/06/150600142. I'll be in touch.``
``Okay.@k@vS06/03/150300051. Sounds good.``
``What?``
``Are you doing all right?``
``Ahahaha -- of course!``
``Good.@k@vS06/06/150600146. I'm hanging up.``
``Okay.@k@vS06/03/150300055. Talk to you soon.``
And with that, our long-overdue sisterly conversation came to a close for the time being.
changeReason: OLD omits that the conversation ended (JPN 終了した); shifts from the JPN's third-person observation to first-person reflection.

TextENGNew: I set down the receiver and let out a breath, and Kasai appeared with tea.
``So, how did it go?``

``Uncle Yoshiroh always had a soft spot for me, remember?@k@vS06/06/150600148. I'm going to come clean to him about leaving the academy@k@vS06/06/150600149. and ask if I can work at one of his shops.``
changeReason: JPN 可愛がってくれた means 'doted on me/was fond of me'; OLD adds 'in favor of me staying' which is not present in the source text.

TextENGNew: ``You'll be seen in public, though.@k@vS06/15/151500087. Uncle Yoshiroh aside, what if another relative spots you?``
changeReason: JPN 人目に触れますよ specifically means 'you'll be seen in public'; OLD's vague 'Are you sure?' omits this concrete concern.

TextENGNew: ``That's what Sis is for.@k@vS06/06/150600151. Once my shifts are set, I'll notify Sis.@k@vS06/06/150600152. She just needs to stay out of sight during my working hours.@k@vS06/06/150600153. Follow?``
Kasai cocked his head slightly.
It really wasn't that complicated.
``It's simple.@k@vS06/06/150600155. If anyone questions me while I'm at work, I just insist that I'm Mion.@k@vS06/06/150600156. Get it now?``
Ordinarily, anyone would want to dismiss such a harebrained scheme.
But Kasai had known us sisters since we were small, and he was well aware of just how effortlessly we could swap places.
``You're certainly audacious... or perhaps cunning is the word...@k@vS06/15/151500089. No, no... I take my hat off to you.``
``That's a compliment, right, Kasai?@k@vS06/06/150600158. Hee hee hee...!``
Kasai wore a look of exasperated amusement.
changeReason: JPN 呆れた顔をして苦笑い means exasperated expression with a wry smile; OLD's 'looked amazed' misses the exasperation and amusement.

TextENGNew: If things truly went sideways, Kasai would bear responsibility too.
...Given the customs of my father's world, a simple apology wouldn't begin to cover it.
That's why Kasai genuinely didn't want me crossing any dangerous bridges.
...He'd helped me escape knowing full well what was at stake. I really needed to find a way to repay this loyal man...
``Yeah, yeah -- leave it to me.@k@vS06/00/152900002. If anyone gives you trouble, Shion-chan, I'll sort them out.``
changeReason: JPN シメとく means to discipline/straighten out, not necessarily 'beat up' as OLD states; also OLD loses the casual 任せとけよ (leave it to me) tone.

TextENGNew: ``Ahaha!@k@vS06/06/150600160. I knew I could count on you, Uncle Yoshiroh!@k@vS06/06/150600161. I was sure you'd come through for me!``
Uncle Yoshiroh kept saying 'leave it to me' and thumped his chest for emphasis.
changeReason: JPN 任せとけを連発 means he repeatedly said 'leave it to me'; OLD omits this repetition detail.

TextENGNew: He seemed genuinely delighted that I'd come to him first.

``It's all good.@k@vS06/00/152900004. What impresses me is your determination to earn your own keep.@k@vS06/00/152900005. If you'd come here begging for handouts, I'd have marched you straight to the main house.``
``What?@k@vS06/06/150600163. Uncle, you're scaring me~!@k@vS06/06/150600164. Ahahahahaha!``
Uncle Yoshiroh was exactly as dependable as I'd hoped.
He understood my situation immediately and promised his full support.
``Manager~, I've got the shift chart.@k@vS06/00/waitA15002. I'll leave it here~.``
One of the shop employees brought in the staff shift schedule.
...The calendar was packed with symbols that meant nothing to an outsider like me.
``...So, Shion-chan, how much are you able to work?``
``It depends on my schedule and Sis's.@k@vS06/06/150600166. Ideally, I'd love to work only on the days I specify.``
``Hey now~, I've never had anyone that brazen walk into a job interview before.@k@vS06/00/152900008. But, well... I know it can't be helped.``
changeReason: JPN ムシのいいこと means 'presumptuous/self-serving demand'; OLD softens to 'a little irresponsible' which loses the uncle's amused reaction.

TextENGNew: Working at all required Mion's cooperation.
In other words, I could only work when both our schedules aligned.
...Uncle Yoshiroh understood these complications, of course.
But I also understood that there wasn't much room in any business for a part-timer audacious enough to dictate her own schedule...
In the end, rather than hiring me as a regular part-timer, Uncle suggested I come in as an office helper.
Since it wasn't a formal hire, there'd be no fixed shifts.
My pay would come from Uncle's own pocket rather than the shop's coffers.
``Ahaha, I don't care about titles.@k@vS06/06/150600168. As long as I can earn enough to cover my living expenses, that's all I need.``
``Then let's go with that.@k@vS06/00/152900010. I am paying you, so I expect solid work while you're here.@k@vS06/00/152900011. Oh, and no calling me 'Uncle' in the office, got it?``
``Yes sir, understood, Man~a~ger!@k@vS06/06/150600170. I may be inexperienced, but please take good care of me!``
changeReason: OLD omits JPN 不束者ですけどよろしくお願いします (humble self-deprecation); NEW preserves this important cultural greeting.

TextENGNew: ``Likewise, pleased to have you aboard.@k@vS06/00/152900013. Bwahahahaha!``
changeReason: JPN こちらこそどうぞよろしく is a standard reciprocal greeting; OLD's 'Good, you get it!' misses the polite tone entirely.

TextENGNew: Once the business talk was settled, Uncle Yoshiroh subjected me to a barrage of questions.
The gist of which was:
What on earth is life like in a prestigious girls' boarding school?!@k He wanted to know everything.
Apparently, all sorts of rumors swirled around the academy I'd been confined to, and everyone was dying of curiosity about what actually went on inside.
I gave him a colorful account of just how far from normal academy life had been, embellishing here and there for dramatic effect.
Uncle was positively delighted to hear all the juicy insider details about the mysterious girls' academy...@k ...The dirty old man.
Once Uncle had thoroughly sated his curiosity about the secret garden of adolescent girls, it was my turn to ask questions.
changeReason: OLD omits the evocative JPN detail 思春期の女の子の花園の秘密 (the secret garden of adolescent girls) which characterizes Uncle's salacious curiosity.

TextENGNew: I needed to know what had happened in town during the two years I'd been locked away at the academy.
changeReason: OLD omits JPN's specific '二年' (two years) and '閉じ込められて' (locked away), which emphasize the duration and nature of Shion's absence.

TextENGNew: Unless I filled in that two-year gap, I wouldn't be able to convincingly pass myself off as 'Mion.'
``Nothing's changed all that dramatically.@k@vS06/00/152900015. It's been pretty sleepy around here since the Dam War ended.``
``I left for the academy not long after they announced the project freeze.@k@vS06/06/150600172. There was still a bit of tension in the air when I was last here.``

``Even after the freeze announcement, the protest alliance was on edge for about a year, insisting the plan was only suspended, not canceled.@k@vS06/00/152900017. They held the official disbandment ceremony the following year.``
changeReason: OLD references 'Onigafuchi Guardians' which isn't in this JPN text (JPN uses 同盟 = 'alliance'); NEW uses 'protest alliance' more accurately.

TextENGNew: ``A grand ceremony right there in the Hinamizawa shrine grounds.@k@vS06/00/152900019. That was the true end of the Dam War.``
``So that was right after I got locked up.@k@vS06/06/150600174. ...With the war over, did everyone just go soft?``

``Bwahahaha!@k@vS06/00/152900021. I bet for you kids, the dam protests felt like one big game of war, didn't they?@k@vS06/00/152900022. Try to spare a thought for the adults who had to drag themselves to the police station every time one of you brats got picked up.``
``Ahahaha!@k@vS06/06/150600176. Well, I can't exactly deny that.@k@vS06/06/150600177. For us kids, the Dam War was practically a festival!``
Uncle seemed both impressed and appalled at how vastly different the Dam War had felt to adults versus children.
changeReason: JPN 感服（呆れ？）explicitly includes both admiration and exasperation with a parenthetical question; OLD only captures exasperation.

TextENGNew: ``...Oh yeah, that infuriating construction foreman.@k@vS06/06/150600180. They still hadn't caught everyone involved in that murder, had they?@k@vS06/06/150600181. Did they ever catch them?``
changeReason: JPN refers to 'that infuriating foreman' (あのクソムカつく現場監督); OLD jumps to 'dismemberment murder' not stated here. Also JPN implies multiple perpetrators (犯人がまだ全員) while OLD uses singular.

TextENGNew: ``Ahh... come to think of it...@k@vS06/00/152900024. No, I don't think the ringleader was ever caught.``
changeReason: JPN 主犯 specifically means 'ringleader/main perpetrator'; OLD's generic 'him' loses this distinction.

TextENGNew: ``And it happened on the night of Watanagashi, the festival honoring Oyashiro-sama, Hinamizawa's guardian deity.@k@vS06/06/150600183. I remember everyone saying it was Oyashiro-sama's curse.@k@vS06/06/150600184. ...That was genuinely scary for us kids.``
``Yeah, yeah -- Oyashiro-sama's curse.@k@vS06/00/152900026. It happened again the following year too.``

``That's right!@k@vS06/06/150600186. That one was terrifying too!@k@vS06/06/150600187. What was it again -- the man who supported the dam died in an accident?@k@vS06/06/150600188. Houjou, wasn't it?@k@vS06/06/150600189. And once again, on the night of Watanagashi.
Everyone was in an uproar -- saying it could only be a curse!@k@vS06/06/150600191. That was genuinely frightening.@k@vS06/06/150600192. It really was.``

The universally despised construction foreman at the dam site had been murdered -- by construction workers, no less -- on the night of the Watanagashi festival.@k Even by itself, it was far too intense an incident for children to process.
changeReason: OLD adds 'dismembered' not in this JPN line; NEW correctly includes 作業員たちの手で (by the construction workers' hands) which OLD omits.

TextENGNew: The fact that part of the body was still missing... only added to the ghost-story mystique in our childish imaginations.
The severed right arm, still wandering in search of the body it belonged to -- in the world of children, that story circulated with absolute credibility.

And since the murder occurred on the very night of the festival honoring Hinamizawa's guardian deity, Oyashiro-sama,@k it took no time at all for the rumor mill to churn it into full-blown occult lore.
But even the most vivid rumor fades after a year.
...Then came the first Watanagashi held after the dam project's indefinite freeze was announced -- a festival that was, for the first time, truly just a festival.
changeReason: OLD says 'we held Watanagashi purely as a festival' which oversimplifies; JPN describes this as the first Watanagashi truly just a festival after the freeze.

TextENGNew: The curse struck again.
The victim was a man named Houjou -- a Hinamizawa resident who had nevertheless supported the dam project. He and his wife died in what was ruled a fall from a cliff while on a trip.
changeReason: OLD adds 'pro-dam activist' characterization and 'It was considered an accident' not in this JPN line; also OLD says singular victim while JPN includes 夫婦共々 (both husband and wife).

TextENGNew: (Technically, I believe the search for the wife's body was still ongoing.@k If I recall correctly, they never did find her...)
Once again, on the night of the Watanagashi festival, the man most deserving of Oyashiro-sama's wrath had fallen.
The talk of Oyashiro-sama's curse, which had been fading, roared back to life in an instant.
Until then, Oyashiro-sama's presence had been a vague and insubstantial thing.
changeReason: JPN 存在は希薄 means Oyashiro-sama's 'existence was thin/insubstantial'; OLD's 'not considered very important' misses the nuance of vagueness.

TextENGNew: In the world of children, at least, it was simply the name of the deity enshrined at the local shrine -- nothing more, nothing less.
There were brats who'd commit sacrilege at the shrine gates, and even I once stuck my hand into the offering box.@k ...Though I put everything back.
changeReason: OLD interprets JPN 失礼を働く (commit disrespect) as specifically 'boys peeing on the archway'; also OLD omits 盗らずに戻した (put it back without stealing).

TextENGNew: The god of the local shrine was just that trivial to us.
But after two consecutive years, no child doubted the reality of Oyashiro-sama's curse anymore.
Everyone genuinely believed in Oyashiro-sama's existence and lived in fear.
The old legends about Oyashiro-sama, which no one had previously cared about, suddenly commanded rapt attention.

We sat in hushed reverence as the village elders spun their musty old tales,@k and we believed -- truly believed -- in Oyashiro-sama's miracles, the same ones any of us would have laughed off not long before.
And Oyashiro-sama's rules turned out to be brutally simple.
Outsiders who encroach upon Hinamizawa will be cursed.
Villagers who leave the village will be cursed.
The definition of 'the village' was frustratingly vague. Did it mean you could never leave Hinamizawa proper? Was Okinomiya still safe?
Was a single step outside the boundary enough to doom you? Would a two-night, three-day hot spring trip be forgiven? We children drew and redrew the line endlessly.

In the end, the consensus landed on: as long as your home was in Hinamizawa or Okinomiya, you were safe.@k And if you had a home there, even extended travel was apparently permissible... a rather odd conclusion.
changeReason: OLD omits JPN ヘンチクリンな決着 (rather odd conclusion), which expresses Shion's bemusement at the arbitrary rules.

TextENGNew: (These were rules made up by children, after all.@k Calling them absurd would be missing the point.)
changeReason: JPN says calling the rules absurd would be missing the point; OLD's 'I know it's weird' doesn't capture this defensive nuance.

TextENGNew: Measured against those rules, my being shipped off to a faraway boarding school certainly seemed to satisfy the conditions for drawing Oyashiro-sama's curse.
changeReason: OLD states definitively 'I'd be cursed'; JPN uses tentative language 条件を満たしているようにも見える (seemed to possibly satisfy the conditions).

TextENGNew: I was at an age where admitting belief in the curse out loud would have been embarrassing, so I kept it to myself.
changeReason: OLD shortens to generic embarrassment; NEW captures the JPN detail about being at an age where belief in curses was embarrassing to admit publicly.

TextENGNew: But deep down, I remember being just a little bit frightened that being sent to a distant land might bring Oyashiro-sama's wrath upon me.
changeReason: OLD truncates to generic 'scared of the curse'; NEW preserves the specific fear of being sent to a distant land triggering the curse.

TextENGNew: ``And then it happened again the year after that.``
``Two years in a row really gets to you, doesn't it?@k@vS06/06/150600194. Even now, I believe in Oyashiro-sama's power.``
``Shion-chan, two years? It didn't stop there. You mean you don't know?``
changeReason: JPN 二年なんてもんじゃない means 'it wasn't just two years'; OLD misses this key correction that it happened more than twice.

TextENGNew: ``Know what?@k@vS06/06/150600196. What are you talking about?``
``...Right, of course.@k@vS06/00/152900030. You went off to the academy just before it happened...@k@vS06/00/152900031. ...You wouldn't know.``
Uncle Yoshiroh's face took on a look of someone who'd said more than he should have.
...As though he was thinking: if she didn't know, maybe it would have been better to keep it that way...
``What is it, Uncle?@k@vS06/06/150600198. You can't bring it up and then just go quiet on me -- that's not fair.``
``...Well, I suppose you're not a little kid anymore.``
``Uncle, if you look at me and still think 'little kid,' you'll need to choose between visiting an optometrist and receiving a roundhouse kick to the face.``
``...Ahh, fine, fine.@k@vS06/00/152900035. I'm not trying to hide it.@k@vS06/00/152900036. It happened again after that.``
changeReason: JPN 別に隠しゃしないよ means 'I'm not really hiding it'; OLD's 'I'll talk' implies reluctant disclosure while JPN implies it wasn't a secret.

TextENGNew: ``Happened? What happened?@k@vS06/06/150600200. ...Uncle, what have you been talking about this whole time?``
A prick.
changeReason: JPN ちくり means 'a prick/sting' (as of a needle, confirmed by later context); OLD's 'Tick' is incorrect.

TextENGNew: When I was a much smaller child.
...Every time I was about to hear something bad, this little prick would come first, without fail.
changeReason: Continuation of ちくり correction; OLD uses 'tick' while NEW correctly uses 'prick' matching the needle metaphor.

TextENGNew: A tiny person living deep inside my chest, warning me in advance that something unpleasant was coming.
...Jabbing my heart with a tiny needle held in its tiny hand.
If this prick came just before my parents called for me, it was guaranteed to be a scolding or bad news.
changeReason: Continuation of ちくり correction from entry 212; OLD's 'tick' should be 'prick' to match the needle metaphor.

TextENGNew: ...Which is why I'd react instantly and bolt.
But the passage of time had dulled my instincts.
The little person inside me had tried to warn me... yet I couldn't muster any response. I just sat there, dazed.
``Well, see...@k@vS06/00/152900038. The year after the Houjous died in that fall...@k@vS06/00/152900039. ...meaning last year.@k@vS06/00/152900040. It happened again.``
``Again...?@k@vS06/06/150600203. ...On the night of Watanagashi?!@k@vS06/06/150600204. Three years running?!@k@vS06/06/150600205. Again?!``

``That's right.@k@vS06/00/152900042. You know the shrine in Hinamizawa?@k@vS06/00/152900043. Furude Shrine.@k@vS06/00/152900044. ...The head priest dropped dead on the night of the festival, just like that.``

``And once again, on the night of Watanagashi.@k@vS06/00/152900047. Then his wife threw herself into the swamp that same night... it was absolute chaos.@k@vS06/00/152900048. The village mayor called an emergency meeting, everything was in an uproar......``
changeReason: OLD restructures and truncates: moves the Watanagashi reference and omits てんやわんや (chaos/uproar) about the village's reaction.
